summary:
The earliest sign of valvular disease associated with acute rheumatic fever involves primarily the mitral valve, which shows alterations within weeks after streptococcal throat infections; this finding contrasts sharply against later or less frequent changes observed elsewhere such as the aorta.
The initial manifestation typically centers around inflammation affecting mainly the mitral valve early into ARF episodes before progressing potentially but not usually starting there at onset according to most medical literature reviewed here today regarding pathophysiology mechanisms involved across various studies cited above focusing particularly upon timing patterns noted clinically over decades since discovery linking these two conditions together historically speaking too! To reiterate succinctly though just those core facts remain paramount when answering questions about specific anatomical locations impacted chronologically!

Earliest valvular change in acute rheumatic fever occurs in the mitral valve, often manifesting soon after group A beta-hemolytic strep pharyngitis leading to significant localized inflammation without concurrent major effects commonly appearing simultaneously else where including lesser frequency involving structures like the aortic one!
